explanation.text: Rosa explains that Stephanie sometimes ignores her or treats her like a little kid when other friends are present. These actions show that Rosa sees Stephanie as sometimes behaving meanly toward a friend.

Rosa joins Kiara while Stephanie talks with the other dancers, hoping Stephanie will notice and feel "left out." Because Rosa wants Stephanie to experience being excluded as she did, the phrase means alone.

The story says Rosa and Stephanie do everything together, then lists riding the bus, eating lunch, taking dance lessons, and attending camp together. This evidence best shows their usual closeness as best friends.

Rosa first copies Stephanie's unfriendly behavior because she wants to get even and hurt her feelings. After seeing Stephanie's sad face, Rosa feels bad and invites both girls, showing a change from exclusion to inclusion.
Stephanie has just been invited to join Rosa and Kiara at the pool when she "brightened" and says she would like that. Her positive reaction indicates that the invitation makes her happier.

Rosa discovers that excluding Stephanie does not feel good, even though she had wanted revenge. Her invitation for Stephanie to join her and Kiara supports the central message that including others is better than trying to get even.
Paragraph 3 asks readers to touch their throats while speaking and notice the tiny, quick shaking of their vocal cords. This hands-on example explains what the vibrations that create sound feel like.
Paragraph 7 says outer space has no air and therefore nothing through which sound waves can move. Without a material to carry the waves, no sound is produced outside a spaceship.

After sound waves make the eardrum vibrate, special nerve hairs farther inside the ear pick up those vibrations. This sequence shows that the hairs next send signals to the brain, which later makes sense of the sounds.
The author describes outer space by saying, "There are no sounds," and then calls that condition "silence." This nearby phrase directly explains that silence means an absence of sound.

The passage traces how vibrations create sound waves, how ears collect them, and how the brain interprets their signals. This repeated explanation supports the main idea that vibrations produce the waves that allow people to hear noises.

The author uses lively examples, simple experiments, and inviting questions to help readers explore vibrations, ears, space, and echoes. This enthusiastic presentation suggests that the author finds the process of understanding sound very interesting.
